meta.title: Poetry Collection — Set 1
meta.subject: English Reading
meta.description: Reading comprehension quiz for the Poetry Collection — understanding the poems 'Me!', 'The Box', and 'Sandcastle'.

questions.question: In the poem 'Me!', what does the speaker say it 'might be fun' to be?
options: A dog or a cat
A fish or a bird
A monkey or a lion
A dragon or a fox
questions.explanation: In 'Me!', the speaker imagines it might be fun to be 'a fish' (and swim the sea) or 'a bird' (and zoom tree to tree).

questions.question: In the poem 'Me!', the speaker says 'I'm happy to be me.'
questions.explanation: Yes! The refrain of 'Me!' is 'I'm happy to be me.' The speaker is glad to be a person — because fish can't skip or read, but people can!

questions.question: In the poem 'The Box', what did the father bring home?
options: A new toy
A big box
A set of books
A pet fish
questions.explanation: In 'The Box,' the poem begins 'When Dad brought home the great big box' — the father brought a large cardboard box.

questions.question: In 'The Box', the child first imagined that the box was a dragon's cave.
questions.explanation: Yes! The child 'crawled inside a cardboard cave and roared a dragon's roar.' The box first became a cave where the child pretended to be a dragon.

questions.question: In 'Sandcastle', what do the waves do to the castle?
options: They make the castle bigger
They bring more shells and stones
They knock at the castle door and melt it away
They help build the walls
questions.explanation: The poem says 'Waves reach up and lick the shore, knocking at our castle door. Soon it all will melt away.' The waves destroy the sandcastle.

questions.question: In 'Me!', a bird can _______ from tree to tree — it moves very fast!
questions.explanation: 'Zoom' means to move very quickly. The poem says a bird can 'zoom from tree to tree' — birds fly very fast.

questions.question: In 'The Box', how did the box change when the father cut a hole in it?
options: It became a spaceship
It changed from a dragon's cave to a fox's den
It became a real house
It changed from a cave to a sandcastle
questions.explanation: When Dad 'carved a little hole into the box,' the poem says 'the cave became a fox's den, and I became a fox.' The imagination changed!

questions.question: In 'Sandcastle', the children use only sand to decorate their castle.
questions.explanation: The children use 'shells and stones to decorate.' They also use 'sticks to make a gate.' It's not just sand — they use natural things they find on the beach.

questions.question: Sort each idea — which poem does it belong to?
items: A box becomes a fox's den
Waves knock at a castle door
A bird can zoom from tree to tree
Shells and stones are used to decorate

categories.label: 'Me!'
answers: A bird can zoom from tree to tree

categories.label: 'The Box'
answers: A box becomes a fox's den

categories.label: 'Sandcastle'
answers: Waves knock at a castle door
Shells and stones are used to decorate

questions.explanation: Each idea belongs to a different poem: 'Me!' talks about fish and birds. 'The Box' talks about the box becoming a den. 'Sandcastle' mentions waves and decorating.

questions.question: What does the last line of 'Sandcastle' suggest?
options: They are angry that the castle is gone
They will never build a sandcastle again
They are invited to come back and build again — building is fun
The waves are mean and should stop
questions.explanation: 'Come and build another day!' invites the reader to come back and build again. Even though the castle melts away, the fun of building can happen again.

questions.question: The child 'crawled inside a _______ cave' — the box was made of cardboard.
questions.hint: The material boxes are made from
questions.explanation: 'Cardboard' is the thick, stiff paper material that boxes are made from. The child pretended the cardboard box was a cave.

questions.question: Arrange the words to form a line from 'Me!':
questions.explanation: Correct line: 'I'm happy to be me.' This is the refrain (repeating line) of the poem 'Me!' — the speaker loves being a person.

questions.question: What is the main message of the poem 'Me!'?
options: It is better to be a fish than a bird
Be happy and proud of who you are
Fish and birds have easy lives
People should learn to swim
questions.explanation: The poem compares being a person to being a fish or bird. Even though those might be fun, people can do special things — skip, run, read. 'I'm happy to be me' means be proud of who you are.

questions.question: In 'The Box', the child uses imagination to turn an ordinary box into many different things.
questions.explanation: The box becomes a cave, then a fox's den, then finally a little house with flowers. The child's imagination transforms the box into different things — this shows the power of creativity.

questions.question: What is the theme of 'Sandcastle'?
options: The ocean is a dangerous place
Enjoy the moment — even beautiful things can be temporary
Sand is a great building material
Children should not play near water
questions.explanation: The sandcastle is built with joy, but the waves melt it away. The poem says 'Come and build another day!' — enjoy the moment, and when it passes, create again. It shows that temporary things can still bring joy.
